claims.normalized_claim: OneCoin was founded in 2014 in Sofia, Bulgaria, by Ruja Ignatova and Karl Sebastian Greenwood, and operated as a multi-level-marketing network: members earned commissions for recruiting new buyers, who purchased tiered 'educational packages' bundled with tokens they were told could 'mine' OneCoins.
evidence_locations: MLM: recruiting commissions; packages + tokens to 'mine'
allowed_wording: OneCoin was founded in 2014 in Sofia, Bulgaria, by Ruja Ignatova and Karl Sebastian Greenwood; it sold tiered 'educational packages' through a recruit-to-earn network.
prohibited_wording: OneCoin was a legitimate cryptocurrency.
claims.notes: Corporate facts stateable. The 'pyramid scheme' label is DOJ's (CLM-0015).

claims.normalized_claim: U.S. prosecutors say — and co-founder Karl Sebastian Greenwood admitted when he pleaded guilty — that OneCoin had no genuine, verifiable blockchain: the company kept its own internal database, and the coin's supply and price were set centrally by OneCoin rather than mined on a distributed ledger. A blockchain developer says OneCoin sought to recruit him to build a blockchain it did not have.
evidence_locations: No real blockchain; central database; supply/price set centrally
Greenwood guilty plea admits scheme facts
Bjercke: ran on SQL; no blockchain existed
allowed_wording: There was no real blockchain. Prosecutors say — and the co-founder admitted in his guilty plea — that the 'coin' was just numbers in a database the company controlled.
prohibited_wording: Ignatova personally faked the blockchain (state as her conduct/fact).
claims.notes: Load-bearing thesis fact. Firmest via Greenwood's plea; Ignatova's personal intent stays ALLEGED. The technologist account is attributed.

claims.normalized_claim: Prosecutors allege that the founders allocated 'coins' that did not exist even on OneCoin's own claimed ledger, internally calling them 'fake coins,' and cite internal messages including a description of mining as 'a story we can sell to the members' and a plan to 'take the money and run.'
evidence_locations: 'a story we can sell to the members'
allowed_wording: Prosecutors say internal messages described the mining as 'a story we can sell to the members.'
prohibited_wording: Ignatova wrote/said X (as settled fact)
quote attributed to a named person without 'prosecutors say'
claims.notes: ALLEGED — always 'emails prosecutors say they exchanged'. Cite SDNY trial record (US v. Scott, 18-cr-318) for on-screen exhibit text; verify verbatim before air.

claims.normalized_claim: According to OneCoin's own records cited by U.S. prosecutors, the company generated 4.037 billion euros in sales revenue between the fourth quarter of 2014 and the fourth quarter of 2016; prosecutors say victims invested over 4 billion US dollars worldwide and that, per OneCoin's promotional materials, over three million people invested.
counterevidence: Independent estimates run higher (~$4.5–5B); victim-count figures vary across releases (3 / 3.4 / 3.5M).
allowed_wording: OneCoin's own records, cited by prosecutors, show more than four billion euros in sales between 2014 and 2016; prosecutors say more than three million people invested over four billion dollars worldwide.
prohibited_wording: exact single victim count as settled fact
claims.notes: Attribute figures to prosecutors / OneCoin's books. Verbatim-confirmed via IRS-CI mirror (SRC-0003).

claims.normalized_claim: Ruja Ignatova cultivated a glamorous 'Dr Ruja' / 'Cryptoqueen' persona and, at a packed OneCoin event at Wembley Arena in London in June 2016, promoted OneCoin as the 'Bitcoin killer,' telling the crowd that within two years no one would speak about Bitcoin anymore.
evidence_locations: 'Bitcoin killer'; 'in two years, nobody will speak about Bitcoin any more'
allowed_wording: At a packed Wembley Arena in June 2016, she called OneCoin the 'Bitcoin killer.'
prohibited_wording: real-person likeness of Ignatova in visuals (invariant 11)
claims.notes: The event is documented; the pitch quote is attributed to BBC reporting. Visuals symbolic only — no likeness.

claims.normalized_claim: According to the BBC's investigation, OneCoin was sold to people who felt they had missed Bitcoin — a ground-floor second chance — and the combination of that fear of missing out with commissions for recruiting turned investors into committed believers who defended OneCoin against doubt.
evidence_locations: Community incentive to believe and silence skeptics
allowed_wording: It was sold to people who felt they'd missed Bitcoin — a second chance not to be too late.
prohibited_wording: mock or blame the victims/believers
claims.notes: Controlling-emotion engine. Attribute analysis to BBC/Bartlett. Dignity: believers are not punchlines.

claims.normalized_claim: Ruja Ignatova is a German national, born in Bulgaria in 1980, who held a doctorate in law (University of Konstanz) and, according to public reporting, had worked at the consultancy McKinsey & Company before OneCoin.
allowed_wording: Ruja Ignatova, a German citizen born in Bulgaria, held a doctorate in law and, by public accounts, had worked at McKinsey.
prohibited_wording: Oxford/McKinsey asserted as primary-documented fact without 'reported'

claims.normalized_claim: Regulators warned about OneCoin before its collapse: the UK's Financial Conduct Authority published a consumer warning in 2016 (later quietly removed in 2017, in circumstances the BBC reported were linked to pressure from the company's lawyers), and on 27 April 2017 Germany's BaFin ordered OneCoin's entities to dismantle their internet-based OneCoins trading system and end sales promotion in Germany immediately.
evidence_locations: BaFin 2017-04-27 order to 'dismantle' the OneCoins trading system
allowed_wording: Regulators warned. In 2016 the UK's FCA publicly warned consumers about OneCoin; in April 2017 Germany's BaFin ordered its system dismantled.
prohibited_wording: state the FCA-removal motive as proven
claims.notes: 'They were warned' beat. BaFin order verbatim-confirmed; FCA-removal motive attributed to BBC.

claims.normalized_claim: U.S. prosecutors in the Southern District of New York filed sealed charges and an arrest warrant for Ruja Ignatova on 12 October 2017; about two weeks later, on or about 25 October 2017, she flew from Sofia, Bulgaria to Athens, Greece and has not been publicly seen since.
allowed_wording: She was charged under seal on October 12, 2017. About two weeks later she flew from Sofia to Athens — and vanished.
prohibited_wording: assert she was 'tipped off' as fact
assert her fate
claims.notes: The central irony, both elements documented. The 'tipped off' link is reported/inference — keep as a question, not a fact.

claims.normalized_claim: According to the U.S. indictment, Ruja Ignatova is charged with wire fraud, securities fraud, and money-laundering conspiracy; the indictment was unsealed on 8 March 2019 when her brother Konstantin Ignatov was arrested at Los Angeles airport. Ignatova has never been arrested or tried, and the allegations have not been tested in court.
allowed_wording: According to the U.S. indictment, Ignatova is charged with fraud and money laundering. She has never been arrested or tried.
prohibited_wording: Ignatova is guilty / a convicted fraudster / she stole
she committed fraud (as fact)
claims.notes: R3 CORE: Ignatova is charged, NOT convicted — always 'alleged/accused/charged'. Never 'guilty'.

claims.normalized_claim: On 30 June 2022 the FBI added Ruja Ignatova to its Ten Most Wanted Fugitives list; the United States is offering a reward of up to 5 million US dollars for information leading to her arrest (raised from 250,000 dollars in June 2024), and as of the latest reporting she remains at large.
evidence_locations: Reward up to $5M (from $250k in 2024-06); still at large
allowed_wording: In June 2022 the FBI put her on its Ten Most Wanted list; the reward now stands at up to five million dollars. She is still out there.

claims.normalized_claim: While Ignatova remains a fugitive, those around her have been convicted: co-founder Karl Sebastian Greenwood pleaded guilty in 2022 and was sentenced in 2023 to 20 years in prison; U.S. lawyer Mark Scott was convicted by a jury in 2019 of laundering about 400 million dollars of OneCoin proceeds and was sentenced in 2024 to 10 years (he has appealed); and Ignatova's brother Konstantin Ignatov pleaded guilty in 2019 and cooperated with prosecutors.
allowed_wording: The people around her have been convicted: the co-founder is serving 20 years; a lawyer who laundered about $400 million got 10.
prohibited_wording: Scott's conviction was 'upheld' on appeal (not confirmed)
claims.notes: PROVEN convictions = the firm ground under the film. Scott appeal: 'has appealed' only.

claims.normalized_claim: OneCoin's victims were ordinary people in dozens of countries who invested savings, inheritances and pension money believing it was a legitimate cryptocurrency; among those who spoke out was Jen McAdam, a Scottish woman who, according to the BBC, lost money she and people close to her had invested and became a leading campaigner for OneCoin's victims.
evidence_locations: Millions of ordinary victims worldwide; real losses
allowed_wording: The victims were ordinary people who trusted what they were told and lost their savings. One of them, Jen McAdam, became a campaigner for the others.
prohibited_wording: blame the victims
invented dialogue for McAdam
exact personal loss figure as audited fact
claims.notes: DIGNITY: moral center. Living person — documented public statements only; attribute personal figures to reporting.

claims.normalized_claim: Ruja Ignatova's fate is unknown: the FBI has said it operates under the assumption that she is still alive, while some press reports have speculated she was killed in 2018 — a claim that remains unconfirmed.
allowed_wording: Her fate is unknown. The FBI says it assumes she is still alive; some reports speculate she was killed in 2018 — unproven.
prohibited_wording: state any fate as fact
name an alleged killer as fact
claims.notes: Unresolved ending = artistic ambiguity, not assertion. Never state a fate.

claims.normalized_claim: The documented record — no genuine blockchain (admitted in a co-founder's guilty plea), explicit regulator warnings, more than four billion dollars taken in, and the convictions of those around her — supports U.S. prosecutors' description of OneCoin as a multibillion-dollar pyramid scheme, and illustrates how a promise of belonging and a second chance can override verification until the warnings no longer matter.
allowed_wording: The story is not that the evidence was hidden. It is that a promise people wanted to believe outran every warning — until belief was all that was left.
prohibited_wording: editorialize beyond the record
assert Ignatova's guilt
claims.notes: Controlling idea; grounded and attributed. The 'pyramid scheme' label is DOJ's.

claims.normalized_claim: In 2026 the U.S. Department of Justice opened a compensation claims process for OneCoin's victims, drawing on a fund of seized assets that is a small fraction of the more than four billion dollars prosecutors say was lost.
allowed_wording: Years later, a compensation process opened for the victims — paying back a fraction of what was lost.
claims.notes: Coda contrast (recovered «« lost). Verify the fund figure before air.

findings: R3 — living, charged-but-unconvicted fugitive (Ignatova) + named associates. PRE-PUBLISH LEGAL REVIEW REQUIRED. Ignatova ALWAYS 'alleged/charged/accused', never 'guilty'. Convicted associates (Greenwood/Scott/Ignatov) stateable; Scott appeal = 'has appealed' (not 'upheld').

DIGNITY: victims are the moral center (CLM-0013) — no schadenfreude; living victims/whistleblowers (McAdam, Bjercke) via documented statements only, no invented dialogue. No real-person likeness of Ignatova or anyone (invariant 11).

Scale figures (CLM-0004) attributed to prosecutors/OneCoin's books; 'no blockchain' (CLM-0002) firmest via Greenwood plea. justice.gov/fbi.gov block bots (403) — verify ★ verbatim quotes in a browser before locking narration. Fate (CLM-0014) never asserted.
